addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll get acquainted with this project by reading the key orienting docs and checking the repo state.

content.content:
1	# CLAUDE.md - Bot Instructions
2	
3	## Project Structure
4	
5	This project uses **two separate jj-git repos**:
6	
7	1. **App repo** (`/` — project root): Contains the application source code.
8	2. **Bot session repo** (`/.claude/`): Contains Claude Code session data.
9	
10	Both repos are managed with `jj` (Jujutsu), which coexists with git.
11	
12	## Repo Paths (relative from project root)
13	
14	- App repo: `.` (project root)
15	- Bot session repo: `.claude`
16	  (symlink from `~/.claude/projects/<path-to-project-root>/.claude`)
17	
18	## Working Directory
19	
20	Prefer staying in the project root. Use `-R` flags or absolute paths
21	to target other directories rather than `cd`. If `cd` seems necessary,
22	discuss with the user first — losing track of cwd causes subtle
23	command failures downstream.
24	
25	When invoking shell commands, prefer the shortest unambiguous path —
26	usually relative to cwd (`ls notes/`, not
27	`ls /home/wink/data/prgs/rust/vc-x1/notes/`). Long absolute paths
28	clutter the transcript without adding information. Out-of-workspace
29	paths (`/tmp/...`, `~/.config/...`) stay absolute. Read/Edit/Write
30	tool args also stay absolute — that's a tool-boundary constraint,
31	not a stylistic choice.
32	
33	## File reads — read the slice you need
34	
35	Long notes files are appended to over time. Read only the
36	slice your task needs; grep or read further on demand.
37	
38	- **`notes/todo.md`** (the routine acquaint read) — first
39	  ~60 lines covers intro + `## In Progress` + the top of the
40	  ranked `## Todo` (priorities, #1 highest). `Read` with
41	  `offset=0, limit=60`. `## Ideas` sits below `## Todo`; read
42	  further only when chasing a lower-ranked entry, an Idea, a
43	  `[N]` ref, or auditing the whole list.
44	- **`notes/todo-backlog.md`** — the long-tail backlog
45	  (lower-priority entries below the ranked `## Todo`). Read
46	  only when picking up a backlog item; grep to locate it
47	  first.
48	- **`notes/bugs.md`** — the bug list. Small; read whole
49	  when triaging a bug or chasing the `## Bugs` pointer in
50	  todo.md.
51	- **`notes/done.md`** + **`notes/chores/chores-NN.md`** —
52	  historical / append-mostly. Scan headings first
53	  (`grep '^## ' notes/chores/chores-NN.md`), then read only
54	  the section you need.
55	
56	**Why:** before the 0.58.0 split, `notes/todo.md` ran ~370
57	lines and grew every cycle. The split moved the backlog and
58	bugs into sibling files so the routine read stays small; the
59	same "slice you need" rule applies to historical files.
60	
61	## Memory
62	
63	Do not use the bot's per-project memory directory
64	(`~/.claude/projects/<path>/memory/`). In a dual-repo setup with
65	CLAUDE.md it provides no capability CLAUDE.md doesn't already cover,
66	and it loses on discoverability:
67	
68	- **CLAUDE.md** — at the repo root, a well-known location for bot
69	  instructions, committed, reviewable, visible to every collaborator
70	  (human or bot).
71	- **Memory directory** — hidden under the user's home, tied to one
72	  machine, invisible to anyone but the bot, never diffed or reviewed.
73	
74	Easy for everyone to find beats convenient for the bot alone. Put
75	durable context in CLAUDE.md (or committed `notes/`) instead.
76	
77	## Speculation marker
78	
79	Durable text the bot writes — CLAUDE.md, `notes/`, commit bodies,
80	chores sections — should stick to observations and direct descriptions
81	of the code or data. If a mechanism, hypothesis, or causal claim
82	enters the text, prefix it with "The bot thinks ..." so a reader can
83	tell the measured from the inferred.
84	
85	**Why:** unmarked speculation reads like evidence, and a future reader
86	(or the bot on a later session) can pick it up as a known fact when
87	it's not. Measured / inferred is a distinction worth keeping visible
88	in the written record.
89	
90	**How to apply:** observations and factual descriptions need no
91	marker. Prefix with "The bot thinks ..." (or a close variant like
92	"The bot's guess is ...") when the claim is a mechanism ("X wins
93	because Y caches better"), a cause ("the drift was due to thermal
94	state"), a prediction ("this should scale linearly"), or any
95	reasoning not directly supported by the data on hand.
96	
97	## jj Basics
98	
99	- `jj st -R .` / `jj st -R .claude` — show working copy status
100	- `jj log -R .` / `jj log -R .claude` — show commit log
101	- `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` — finalize working copy into a commit
102	- `jj describe -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` — set description without committing
103	- `jj git push --bookmark <name> -R <repo>` — push a bookmark (no
104	  `--allow-new` flag; jj pushes new bookmarks without special flags)
105	- In jj, the working copy (@) is always a mutable commit being edited.
106	  `jj commit` finalizes it and creates a new empty working copy on top.
107	- The `.claude` repo always has uncommitted changes during an active
108	  session because session data updates continuously.
109	- **Post-amend `jj new`** — `jj edit <rev>`,
110	  `jj rebase -r <rev> -d ...`, and `jj squash --into <rev>`
111	  all leave `@` on the rewritten commit rather than on an
112	  empty change above. The most common case is the close-out
113	  push for a [Merge (non-FF)](notes/cycle-protocol.md#pushing) shape (`jj rebase
114	  -r <tip> -d <prev> -d <sub-tip>` leaves `@` on the new
115	  merge). In a colocated jj+git repo, git then detaches
116	  HEAD at the commit's first parent, so `git status`,
117	  gitk, and IDE diff views read the commit's content as
118	  uncommitted relative to HEAD. Follow up with `jj new` to
119	  create an empty `@` above; HEAD shifts to the commit and
120	  the working tree reads clean.
121	
122	## Prose form
123	
124	Long-lived prose on this project follows one basic shape: a short intro
125	that explains the *why* or the high-level *what*, then a `-` bullet
126	list for the details. Wrap lines at ≤72 cols (bullet continuations
127	indent two spaces). Avoid wall-of-prose paragraphs — they hide the
128	structure that bullets make scannable.
129	
130	Surfaces that use this shape:
131	
132	- Module / function / struct / field doc comments in `.rs` files —
133	  see [Doc comments](#doc-comments-on-every-file-function-and-method).
134	- Commit message bodies (both app-repo and session-repo). The
135	  ≤50-col title is the commit-specific add-on; see
136	  [Per-commit flow](notes/cycle-protocol.md#per-commit-flow).
137	- Chore descriptions in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` — see
138	  [Chores section content](#chores-section-content--no-edit-list-git-is-the-record).
139	- Todo and Done entries in `notes/todo.md` when an entry needs more
140	  than one line of detail. Pure one-liners are still fine.
141	
142	Bullet *content* differs by surface:
143	
144	- **Commit bodies** — bullets are file-by-file: one bullet per file
145	  changed, file plus a one-line gist (e.g.
146	  `README.md: new Overview intro`). Source of truth for the
147	  mechanical edit list.
148	- **Chores / todo / done** — bullets are conceptual (design points,
149	  structural notes, the "what landed and why" at a notch above
150	  file-list granularity). Never a copy of the commit's edit list —
151	  see [Chores section content](#chores-section-content--no-edit-list-git-is-the-record).
152	- **Doc comments** — bullets are whatever structure fits (fields,
153	  cases, invariants).
154	
155	**Problem + plan shape.** `## In Progress` cycle blocks,
156	chores section intros, and `## Todo` entries use a sharper
157	form of the same shape:
158	
159	- **Problem statement** (the why) — one or two sentences;
160	  don't pad with intent, don't restate the plan.
161	- **Plan bullets** (the what/when) — formality differs by
162	  surface:
163	  - In Progress / chores: numbered ladder (`-N` suffixes,
164	    `(current)` / `(done)` markers) — a committed sequence.
165	  - Todo entries: rough informal bullets, no numbering;
166	    formalized only when the entry is picked up into a
167	    cycle.
168	
169	**Semicolons inside bullets.** A bullet that joins
170	multiple clauses with semicolons (`A; B; C`) is a list
171	hiding inside running prose — break the clauses into
172	sub-bullets so the structure shows. Semicolons in running
173	prose (intro paragraphs, sentence-joins) are fine. Not
174	absolute: very short clauses or tight pairs can stay
175	joined inside a bullet when breaking would be more noise
176	than signal.
177	
178	## Notes file conventions
179	
180	Conventions the bot follows when writing notes files
181	(`notes/todo.md`, `notes/todo-backlog.md`, `notes/bugs.md`,
182	`notes/chores/chores-NN.md`, `notes/done.md`). One source
183	of truth lives here; [`notes/README.md`](notes/README.md)
184	points back.
185	
186	### Notes references
187	
188	Reference *citations* are double-bracketed so the brackets render
189	— `[[N]]`, or `[[2]],[[3]]` for several (comma-separated, not
190	`[2,3]` or `[[2]][[3]]`). The `[N]:` definitions in a file's
191	`# References` section and inline `[text](url)` / `[text](#anchor)`
192	links stay single-bracketed.
193	
194	### Reference numbering
195	
196	Every note file (`todo.md`, `todo-backlog.md`, `bugs.md`,
197	`chores-NN.md`, `done.md`) keeps a file-local `# References`
198	section at the bottom. Reference numbers are scoped to that
199	file — `[1]` in `chores-07.md` and `[1]` in `chores-01.md`
200	are independent slots that may point at completely different
201	URLs. New chores files start their numbering at `[1]`.
202	
203	Treat `[N]` like a **footnote**: the number is a local slot, only
204	meaningful within its file's `# References`. So a `[N]` *citation*
205	(bare `[N]`, or doubled `[[N]]`) never reuses another file's number; to cite a target a sibling
206	file references, pick your own next-local slot and define it
207	(the same target may carry a different number in each file). A
208	`[N]` *inside a code span* (`` `[72]` ``) is different — that's
209	a quoted identifier: literal text naming a ref-key (often from
210	another file's namespace), data, not a citation; it needs no
211	definition here. To point at a section of another file from
212	prose, use an inline link with an anchor —
213	`[that section](../chores-07.md#…)` — not a bare number.
214	
215	A `chores-NN.md` `# References` entry is usually a
216	`/notes/<file>.md#anchor` (or `/ARCHITECTURE.md`) path, but may
217	also be a **commit reference** —
218	`[N]: <commit-url-with-12-hex-SHA> "<full-40-hex-SHA>"`, cited by
219	a section's `Commits:` line. See
220	[Chores commit references](#chores-commit-references) for the
221	why and the exact shape.
222	
223	A file's `# References` can be **re-packed** to a contiguous
224	`[1]..[N]` in first-citation-appearance order — walk the file's
225	prose in document order (`todo.md`: `## Todo` then `## Done`;
226	`chores-NN.md`: top to bottom) and number refs as their first
227	`[[N]]` citation appears. This is a file-local rewrite — only
228	that file's `[[N]]` citations and `[N]:` definitions move; every
229	target and sibling file is untouched. A `[[N]]` inside a `` ` ``
230	code span is a literal token, not a citation, and is left alone.
231	Do it opportunistically (when the namespace has drifted enough to
232	annoy), not on a schedule: `todo.md` fragments fastest (entries
233	land and get pruned every cycle) and is the usual candidate;
234	`chores-NN.md` / `done.md` are append-mostly and only need it
235	after an unusual event (e.g. a bulk retrofit that allocated slots
236	out of document order).
237	
238	### Markdown anchor links
239	
240	GitHub anchor algorithm: lowercase, strip non-alphanumeric
241	characters in place, map remaining spaces to hyphens 1-for-1. Do
242	**not** collapse adjacent whitespace — so `a + b` → `a--b` (spaces
243	on both sides of `+`), but `a: b` → `a-b` (only trailing space on
244	`:`). General markdown reference:
245	[markdownguide.org](https://www.markdownguide.org). GitHub
246	publishes no official spec for auto-generated anchors; the
247	de-facto reference implementation is
248	[github-slugger](https://github.com/Flet/github-slugger).
249	
250	### Todo format
251	
252	`todo.md` is organized into `## In Progress`, `## Todo`
253	(strict priority rank, #1 highest; long-tail backlog in
254	[todo-backlog.md](notes/todo-backlog.md)), `## Ideas`,
255	`## Bugs` (pointer to [bugs.md](notes/bugs.md)), and
256	`## Done` sections. Each item is a short description with
257	reference links to more detail.
258	
259	`## Todo` and `## Bugs` entries carry explicit `1.` `2.` …
260	numbers in the source. For `## Todo` the number is its
261	**priority rank** (#1 highest, descending); for `## Bugs`
262	it's just an index. They're for grepping and at-a-glance
263	"let's do #1" — **not stable IDs**: reorder (to
264	reprioritize), insert, or delete freely, then
265	`vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run` renumbers and normalizes
266	continuation-line indent, so any given number is positional.
267	**To refer to a Todo durably, name it by its title — a
268	plain, greppable text mention.** Not its number (positional,
269	renumbered) and not a markdown link: a numbered list item has
270	no anchor to link to.
271	
272	Numbering helps a human orient in a long list but makes links
273	difficult and fragile — especially an external reference
274	pointing in, which can't be auto-fixed when the list
275	renumbers. A robust fix (a number-free anchor, or a
276	number-tolerant dereference that matches the title slug and
277	wildcards the numeric prefix — a GitHub slug like `5-foo` is
278	encoded, not opaque, so the title is recoverable) is a
279	`validate-numbering` design question, out of scope here.
280	
281	`vc-x1 fix-todo` alone only previews; `vc-x1 validate-todo`
282	is the read-only check. The `## Done` section keeps `-`
283	bullets — items aren't referenced by number once completed.
284	
285	Example shape:
286	
287	```
288	# Todo
289	- Add new feature X [details](features.md#feature-x)
290	- Fix bug Y [[1]]
291	
292	# Done
293	- Fixed issue Z [[2]],[[3]]
294	
295	[1]: bugs.md#bug-y
296	[2]: issues.md#issue-z
297	[3]: fixes.md#fix-z
298	```
299	
300	### Retiring Done entries
301	
302	`todo.md`'s `## Done` section is a rolling buffer of recently
303	shipped work, not a permanent log. Move entries into `done.md`
304	at two natural beats:
305	
306	- **Closing a ladder** — when the final `X.Y.Z` (no suffix)
307	  commit ships, decide which prior entries are no longer
308	  needed for nearby context and migrate them.
309	- **Opening a new ladder** — at `X.Y.Z-0`, do the same sweep
310	  before bumping the version.
311	
312	Migration mechanics:
313	
314	- Move the bullet itself from `todo.md > ## Done` to
315	  `done.md` (preserving the original ref number).
316	- Copy any references the moved entries cite into
317	  `done.md`'s `# References` section (those refs are
318	  file-local, so coexisting with `todo.md`'s namespace is
319	  fine).
320	- Prune any references in `todo.md > # References` no longer
321	  cited by anything in `## In Progress` / `## Todo` /
322	  `## Done`. This frees the numbers for future reuse.
323	
324	## Chores conventions
325	
326	### Headings and entries that record a commit
327	
328	A `chores-NN.md` `##` section header that records a specific
329	commit, the matching `todo.md > ## Done` entry, and any `[N]`
330	reference to that section all use **exactly that commit's
331	title** — `<type>: <desc> (<version>)`, the same string the
332	commit gets (see [Per-commit flow](notes/cycle-protocol.md#per-commit-flow)).
333	E.g. the chores header `## refactor: port push to Context
334	(0.48.0-6)` and the Done line `- refactor: port push to Context
335	(0.48.0-6) [[3]]`. The `## Done` entry uses the close-out
336	commit's title.
337	
338	This does **not** apply to organizational headings (`## Todo`,
339	`## In Progress`, `# References`) or to design `###` subsections
340	inside a chores section — those are named for whatever fits.
341	Among the commit-recording ones, exact match is the strong
342	default (nothing absolute): a near-miss just makes it harder to
343	line a record up with its commit.
344	
345	A commit-recording header is provisional while the work is in
346	progress; the *last* edit before `vc-x1 push` syncs it — and the
347	`## Done` entry / `[N]` anchor for that commit — to the final
348	commit title. See [Markdown anchor links](#markdown-anchor-links)
349	for the slug algorithm; the pre-commit checklist catches a
350	dangling `#anchor`, and a future `vc-x1 validate-repo` should too
351	(and should verify the recorded title matches the commit).
352	
353	Existing sections and `## Done` entries (most of chores-01..09;
354	the pre-`0.48.1` `## Done` lines) predate this and keep their
355	free-form text; the convention applies going forward — `0.48.2`
356	converts the `0.48.1` section + its Done entry as the worked
357	example.
358	
359	### Chores section content — no edit list; git is the record
360	
361	A chores section is: a `Commits:` line (first line under the
362	header — see below), then [Prose form](#prose-form) (intro +
363	bullets) for what landed and why, and any `###` design
364	subsections. Bullets here are **conceptual** — design points,
365	structural notes — never a per-file edit list. That lives in the
366	commit message body, which is the source of truth for "what
367	changed mechanically" (immutable, `git show`-able, naturally
368	scoped to the commit). The chores section is the source of truth
369	for the design thinking; the two cross-link, neither restates the
370	other.
371	
372	When the intro starts wanting to explain a mechanism,
373	hypothesis, or wrinkle, don't inflate it — promote that to its
374	own `###` subsection inside the same `chores-NN.md`. If the
375	wrinkle is a live design concern (something that *should*
376	change, not just be recorded), also add a `notes/todo.md` item
377	with a `[N]` ref pointing at that subsection (todo→chores is the
378	normal ref direction).
379	
380	**Why:** a chores edit list and the commit body were specified
381	to be the same content in two places — and detail written twice
382	drifts. Git owns the mechanical record; chores owns the
383	narrative; `Commits:` links them.
384	
385	### Chores commit references
386	
387	The first line under a chores section header is a `Commits:`
388	line citing the git commit(s) that section records:
389	
390	```
391	## refactor: port push to Context (0.48.0-6)
392	
393	Commits: [[3]]
394	
395	<intro paragraph...>
396	```
397	
398	`Commits:` uses the file-local `[N]` reference machinery (see
399	[Reference numbering](#reference-numbering)),
400	**double-bracketed** so the brackets render — `Commits: [[3]]`,
401	or `Commits: [[3]],[[5]]` for several. (`[[3]]` shows as a
402	literal `[`, the `[3]` link, then a literal `]`; the inner
403	`[3]` resolves against its `[3]:` definition — CommonMark /
404	GitHub / VS Code all do this.) The `# References` definition
405	puts the **commit URL** as the destination, with the **full
406	40-hex SHA** in the title slot:
407	
408	```
409	[3]: https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1/commit/<12-hex> "<40-hex>"
410	```
411	
412	- The 12-hex short SHA in the URL keeps it short; GitHub /
413	  GitLab resolve a unique prefix to the canonical commit page
414	  (GitLab's path has a `/-/` before `commit/`).
415	- The full SHA in the title is host-agnostic and unambiguous —
416	  it survives a repo host change, `git show <40-hex>` works in
417	  any clone, and external tooling scraping the notes (a
418	  database, say) gets the canonical identifier.
419	
420	**Timing.** The commit doesn't exist when its chores section is
421	written, so the `Commits:` line is **backfilled when the next
422	change to that repo is started** — the cycle-start step grabs
423	the just-pushed commit's URL + SHA and fills it in. The single
424	newest section is briefly `Commits:`-less; that's fine — the
425	commit itself is the record, and `git log --grep "(X.Y.Z)"`
426	finds it.
427	
428	## Cycle Protocol
429	
430	Every change runs as a **cycle**: Preparation (`X.Y.Z-0`) →
431	Work commits (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …) → Close-out (bare
432	`X.Y.Z`). Each commit runs through a per-commit flow whose
433	cargo cycle is `cargo fmt`, `cargo clippy --all-targets
434	-- -D warnings`, `cargo test`, `cargo install --path .
435	--locked` (skip-able for purely-docs commits; mandatory at
436	close-out). The full protocol — numbering, per-commit
437	flow, reviewing changes, close-out, pushing, ochid
438	trailers, sub-cycles — lives in
439	[`notes/cycle-protocol.md`](notes/cycle-protocol.md). Read
440	it before any commit work.
441	
442	
443	## Code Conventions
444	
445	### Doc comments on every file, function, and method
446	
447	Every `.rs` file must begin with a `//!` module docstring. Every
448	function and method must have a `///` doc comment. Keep them brief —
449	one sentence of purpose is often enough; the discipline is that the
450	comment exists, not that it be long. Doc comments follow the
451	[Prose form](#prose-form) shape (intro + bullets).
452	
453	This is a deliberate override of the generic "write no comments"
454	default that applies to inline `//` comments. Doc comments on the
455	module / item surface are expected; inline explanatory comments
456	inside function bodies remain discouraged unless they capture a
457	non-obvious WHY.
458	
459	**Clap-derive args:** doc comments on `#[arg(...)]` fields drive
460	`--help` output. Clap reflows by default and collapses bullets
461	into running prose. Add `#[arg(verbatim_doc_comment, ...)]` on
462	any field whose doc comment uses bullets so each `- …` lands on
463	its own line in the rendered help.
464	
465	### `// OK: …` comments on `unwrap*` calls (Rust)
466	
467	Non-test code that calls `.unwrap()`, `.unwrap_or(…)`,
468	`.unwrap_or_default()`, or `.unwrap_or_else(…)` must have a trailing
469	`// OK: …` comment that justifies why the call is acceptable.
470	
471	- `// OK: <specific reason>` — document the real precondition,
472	  invariant, or domain reason. Preferred whenever the reason isn't
473	  self-evident.
474	- `// OK: obvious` — the default is self-evident from context (e.g.
475	  `desc.lines().next().unwrap_or("")` — empty desc → empty title).
476	
477	Bare `// OK` is not used (reads like a truncated comment).
478	Abbreviations (e.g. `SE`) are not used because they require a decoder
479	ring for readers seeing the code out of context.
480	
481	For provably-unreachable `.unwrap()` calls, also prefix with
482	`#[allow(clippy::unwrap_used)]` so the site stays silent if we enable
483	the project-wide `clippy::unwrap_used` lint later.
484	
485	```rust
486	let max = stderr_level.unwrap_or(LevelFilter::Info); // OK: default verbosity when -v/-vv absent
487	let first_line = desc.lines().next().unwrap_or("");  // OK: obvious
488	
489	match matches.len() {
490	    1 => {
491	        #[allow(clippy::unwrap_used)]
492	        // OK: `1 =>` arm guarantees matches.len() == 1
493	        Ok(TitleMatch::One(matches.into_iter().next().unwrap()))
494	    }
495	    // ...
496	}
497	```
498	
499	Tests (`#[cfg(test)]`) are exempt — panicking on setup failure is the
500	correct test behavior.
501	
502	### Ask for clarification on ambiguous input
503	
504	When user input is ambiguous or missing necessary detail, stop and
505	ask a specific question. Do not proceed on a guess and hope the
506	result lands right — a clarifying question costs a few seconds;
507	redoing misaligned work costs much more.
508	
509	### Recognize when stuck
510	
511	If a simple task has eaten 5+ minutes of thinking or back-and-forth
512	without progress, stop. Summarize what's blocking — unclear
513	requirements, unfamiliar API surface, conflicting signals — and ask.
514	Continued flailing produces worse outcomes than a direct "I'm stuck
515	on X."
516

# CLAUDE.md - Bot Instructions

## Project Structure

This project uses **two separate jj-git repos**:

1. **App repo** (`/` — project root): Contains the application source code.
2. **Bot session repo** (`/.claude/`): Contains Claude Code session data.

Both repos are managed with `jj` (Jujutsu), which coexists with git.

## Repo Paths (relative from project root)

- App repo: `.` (project root)
- Bot session repo: `.claude`
  (symlink from `~/.claude/projects/<path-to-project-root>/.claude`)

## Working Directory

Prefer staying in the project root. Use `-R` flags or absolute paths
to target other directories rather than `cd`. If `cd` seems necessary,
discuss with the user first — losing track of cwd causes subtle
command failures downstream.

When invoking shell commands, prefer the shortest unambiguous path —
usually relative to cwd (`ls notes/`, not
`ls /home/wink/data/prgs/rust/vc-x1/notes/`). Long absolute paths
clutter the transcript without adding information. Out-of-workspace
paths (`/tmp/...`, `~/.config/...`) stay absolute. Read/Edit/Write
tool args also stay absolute — that's a tool-boundary constraint,
not a stylistic choice.

## File reads — read the slice you need

Long notes files are appended to over time. Read only the
slice your task needs; grep or read further on demand.

- **`notes/todo.md`** (the routine acquaint read) — first
  ~60 lines covers intro + `## In Progress` + the top of the
  ranked `## Todo` (priorities, #1 highest). `Read` with
  `offset=0, limit=60`. `## Ideas` sits below `## Todo`; read
  further only when chasing a lower-ranked entry, an Idea, a
  `[N]` ref, or auditing the whole list.
- **`notes/todo-backlog.md`** — the long-tail backlog
  (lower-priority entries below the ranked `## Todo`). Read
  only when picking up a backlog item; grep to locate it
  first.
- **`notes/bugs.md`** — the bug list. Small; read whole
  when triaging a bug or chasing the `## Bugs` pointer in
  todo.md.
- **`notes/done.md`** + **`notes/chores/chores-NN.md`** —
  historical / append-mostly. Scan headings first
  (`grep '^## ' notes/chores/chores-NN.md`), then read only
  the section you need.

**Why:** before the 0.58.0 split, `notes/todo.md` ran ~370
lines and grew every cycle. The split moved the backlog and
bugs into sibling files so the routine read stays small; the
same "slice you need" rule applies to historical files.

## Memory

Do not use the bot's per-project memory directory
(`~/.claude/projects/<path>/memory/`). In a dual-repo setup with
CLAUDE.md it provides no capability CLAUDE.md doesn't already cover,
and it loses on discoverability:

- **CLAUDE.md** — at the repo root, a well-known location for bot
  instructions, committed, reviewable, visible to every collaborator
  (human or bot).
- **Memory directory** — hidden under the user's home, tied to one
  machine, invisible to anyone but the bot, never diffed or reviewed.

Easy for everyone to find beats convenient for the bot alone. Put
durable context in CLAUDE.md (or committed `notes/`) instead.

## Speculation marker

Durable text the bot writes — CLAUDE.md, `notes/`, commit bodies,
chores sections — should stick to observations and direct descriptions
of the code or data. If a mechanism, hypothesis, or causal claim
enters the text, prefix it with "The bot thinks ..." so a reader can
tell the measured from the inferred.

**Why:** unmarked speculation reads like evidence, and a future reader
(or the bot on a later session) can pick it up as a known fact when
it's not. Measured / inferred is a distinction worth keeping visible
in the written record.

**How to apply:** observations and factual descriptions need no
marker. Prefix with "The bot thinks ..." (or a close variant like
"The bot's guess is ...") when the claim is a mechanism ("X wins
because Y caches better"), a cause ("the drift was due to thermal
state"), a prediction ("this should scale linearly"), or any
reasoning not directly supported by the data on hand.

## jj Basics

- `jj st -R .` / `jj st -R .claude` — show working copy status
- `jj log -R .` / `jj log -R .claude` — show commit log
- `jj commit -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` — finalize working copy into a commit
- `jj describe -m "title" -m "body" -R <repo>` — set description without committing
- `jj git push --bookmark <name> -R <repo>` — push a bookmark (no
  `--allow-new` flag; jj pushes new bookmarks without special flags)
- In jj, the working copy (@) is always a mutable commit being edited.
  `jj commit` finalizes it and creates a new empty working copy on top.
- The `.claude` repo always has uncommitted changes during an active
  session because session data updates continuously.
- **Post-amend `jj new`** — `jj edit <rev>`,
  `jj rebase -r <rev> -d ...`, and `jj squash --into <rev>`
  all leave `@` on the rewritten commit rather than on an
  empty change above. The most common case is the close-out
  push for a [Merge (non-FF)](notes/cycle-protocol.md#pushing) shape (`jj rebase
  -r <tip> -d <prev> -d <sub-tip>` leaves `@` on the new
  merge). In a colocated jj+git repo, git then detaches
  HEAD at the commit's first parent, so `git status`,
  gitk, and IDE diff views read the commit's content as
  uncommitted relative to HEAD. Follow up with `jj new` to
  create an empty `@` above; HEAD shifts to the commit and
  the working tree reads clean.

## Prose form

Long-lived prose on this project follows one basic shape: a short intro
that explains the *why* or the high-level *what*, then a `-` bullet
list for the details. Wrap lines at ≤72 cols (bullet continuations
indent two spaces). Avoid wall-of-prose paragraphs — they hide the
structure that bullets make scannable.

Surfaces that use this shape:

- Module / function / struct / field doc comments in `.rs` files —
  see [Doc comments](#doc-comments-on-every-file-function-and-method).
- Commit message bodies (both app-repo and session-repo). The
  ≤50-col title is the commit-specific add-on; see
  [Per-commit flow](notes/cycle-protocol.md#per-commit-flow).
- Chore descriptions in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` — see
  [Chores section content](#chores-section-content--no-edit-list-git-is-the-record).
- Todo and Done entries in `notes/todo.md` when an entry needs more
  than one line of detail. Pure one-liners are still fine.

Bullet *content* differs by surface:

- **Commit bodies** — bullets are file-by-file: one bullet per file
  changed, file plus a one-line gist (e.g.
  `README.md: new Overview intro`). Source of truth for the
  mechanical edit list.
- **Chores / todo / done** — bullets are conceptual (design points,
  structural notes, the "what landed and why" at a notch above
  file-list granularity). Never a copy of the commit's edit list —
  see [Chores section content](#chores-section-content--no-edit-list-git-is-the-record).
- **Doc comments** — bullets are whatever structure fits (fields,
  cases, invariants).

**Problem + plan shape.** `## In Progress` cycle blocks,
chores section intros, and `## Todo` entries use a sharper
form of the same shape:

- **Problem statement** (the why) — one or two sentences;
  don't pad with intent, don't restate the plan.
- **Plan bullets** (the what/when) — formality differs by
  surface:
  - In Progress / chores: numbered ladder (`-N` suffixes,
    `(current)` / `(done)` markers) — a committed sequence.
  - Todo entries: rough informal bullets, no numbering;
    formalized only when the entry is picked up into a
    cycle.

**Semicolons inside bullets.** A bullet that joins
multiple clauses with semicolons (`A; B; C`) is a list
hiding inside running prose — break the clauses into
sub-bullets so the structure shows. Semicolons in running
prose (intro paragraphs, sentence-joins) are fine. Not
absolute: very short clauses or tight pairs can stay
joined inside a bullet when breaking would be more noise
than signal.

## Notes file conventions

Conventions the bot follows when writing notes files
(`notes/todo.md`, `notes/todo-backlog.md`, `notes/bugs.md`,
`notes/chores/chores-NN.md`, `notes/done.md`). One source
of truth lives here; [`notes/README.md`](notes/README.md)
points back.

### Notes references

Reference *citations* are double-bracketed so the brackets render
— `[[N]]`, or `[[2]],[[3]]` for several (comma-separated, not
`[2,3]` or `[[2]][[3]]`). The `[N]:` definitions in a file's
`# References` section and inline `[text](url)` / `[text](#anchor)`
links stay single-bracketed.

### Reference numbering

Every note file (`todo.md`, `todo-backlog.md`, `bugs.md`,
`chores-NN.md`, `done.md`) keeps a file-local `# References`
section at the bottom. Reference numbers are scoped to that
file — `[1]` in `chores-07.md` and `[1]` in `chores-01.md`
are independent slots that may point at completely different
URLs. New chores files start their numbering at `[1]`.

Treat `[N]` like a **footnote**: the number is a local slot, only
meaningful within its file's `# References`. So a `[N]` *citation*
(bare `[N]`, or doubled `[[N]]`) never reuses another file's number; to cite a target a sibling
file references, pick your own next-local slot and define it
(the same target may carry a different number in each file). A
`[N]` *inside a code span* (`` `[72]` ``) is different — that's
a quoted identifier: literal text naming a ref-key (often from
another file's namespace), data, not a citation; it needs no
definition here. To point at a section of another file from
prose, use an inline link with an anchor —
`[that section](../chores-07.md#…)` — not a bare number.

A `chores-NN.md` `# References` entry is usually a
`/notes/<file>.md#anchor` (or `/ARCHITECTURE.md`) path, but may
also be a **commit reference** —
`[N]: <commit-url-with-12-hex-SHA> "<full-40-hex-SHA>"`, cited by
a section's `Commits:` line. See
[Chores commit references](#chores-commit-references) for the
why and the exact shape.

A file's `# References` can be **re-packed** to a contiguous
`[1]..[N]` in first-citation-appearance order — walk the file's
prose in document order (`todo.md`: `## Todo` then `## Done`;
`chores-NN.md`: top to bottom) and number refs as their first
`[[N]]` citation appears. This is a file-local rewrite — only
that file's `[[N]]` citations and `[N]:` definitions move; every
target and sibling file is untouched. A `[[N]]` inside a `` ` ``
code span is a literal token, not a citation, and is left alone.
Do it opportunistically (when the namespace has drifted enough to
annoy), not on a schedule: `todo.md` fragments fastest (entries
land and get pruned every cycle) and is the usual candidate;
`chores-NN.md` / `done.md` are append-mostly and only need it
after an unusual event (e.g. a bulk retrofit that allocated slots
out of document order).

### Markdown anchor links

GitHub anchor algorithm: lowercase, strip non-alphanumeric
characters in place, map remaining spaces to hyphens 1-for-1. Do
**not** collapse adjacent whitespace — so `a + b` → `a--b` (spaces
on both sides of `+`), but `a: b` → `a-b` (only trailing space on
`:`). General markdown reference:
[markdownguide.org](https://www.markdownguide.org). GitHub
publishes no official spec for auto-generated anchors; the
de-facto reference implementation is
[github-slugger](https://github.com/Flet/github-slugger).

### Todo format

`todo.md` is organized into `## In Progress`, `## Todo`
(strict priority rank, #1 highest; long-tail backlog in
[todo-backlog.md](notes/todo-backlog.md)), `## Ideas`,
`## Bugs` (pointer to [bugs.md](notes/bugs.md)), and
`## Done` sections. Each item is a short description with
reference links to more detail.

`## Todo` and `## Bugs` entries carry explicit `1.` `2.` …
numbers in the source. For `## Todo` the number is its
**priority rank** (#1 highest, descending); for `## Bugs`
it's just an index. They're for grepping and at-a-glance
"let's do #1" — **not stable IDs**: reorder (to
reprioritize), insert, or delete freely, then
`vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run` renumbers and normalizes
continuation-line indent, so any given number is positional.
**To refer to a Todo durably, name it by its title — a
plain, greppable text mention.** Not its number (positional,
renumbered) and not a markdown link: a numbered list item has
no anchor to link to.

Numbering helps a human orient in a long list but makes links
difficult and fragile — especially an external reference
pointing in, which can't be auto-fixed when the list
renumbers. A robust fix (a number-free anchor, or a
number-tolerant dereference that matches the title slug and
wildcards the numeric prefix — a GitHub slug like `5-foo` is
encoded, not opaque, so the title is recoverable) is a
`validate-numbering` design question, out of scope here.

`vc-x1 fix-todo` alone only previews; `vc-x1 validate-todo`
is the read-only check. The `## Done` section keeps `-`
bullets — items aren't referenced by number once completed.

Example shape:

```
# Todo
- Add new feature X [details](features.md#feature-x)
- Fix bug Y [[1]]

# Done
- Fixed issue Z [[2]],[[3]]

[1]: bugs.md#bug-y
[2]: issues.md#issue-z
[3]: fixes.md#fix-z
```

### Retiring Done entries

`todo.md`'s `## Done` section is a rolling buffer of recently
shipped work, not a permanent log. Move entries into `done.md`
at two natural beats:

- **Closing a ladder** — when the final `X.Y.Z` (no suffix)
  commit ships, decide which prior entries are no longer
  needed for nearby context and migrate them.
- **Opening a new ladder** — at `X.Y.Z-0`, do the same sweep
  before bumping the version.

Migration mechanics:

- Move the bullet itself from `todo.md > ## Done` to
  `done.md` (preserving the original ref number).
- Copy any references the moved entries cite into
  `done.md`'s `# References` section (those refs are
  file-local, so coexisting with `todo.md`'s namespace is
  fine).
- Prune any references in `todo.md > # References` no longer
  cited by anything in `## In Progress` / `## Todo` /
  `## Done`. This frees the numbers for future reuse.

## Chores conventions

### Headings and entries that record a commit

A `chores-NN.md` `##` section header that records a specific
commit, the matching `todo.md > ## Done` entry, and any `[N]`
reference to that section all use **exactly that commit's
title** — `<type>: <desc> (<version>)`, the same string the
commit gets (see [Per-commit flow](notes/cycle-protocol.md#per-commit-flow)).
E.g. the chores header `## refactor: port push to Context
(0.48.0-6)` and the Done line `- refactor: port push to Context
(0.48.0-6) [[3]]`. The `## Done` entry uses the close-out
commit's title.

This does **not** apply to organizational headings (`## Todo`,
`## In Progress`, `# References`) or to design `###` subsections
inside a chores section — those are named for whatever fits.
Among the commit-recording ones, exact match is the strong
default (nothing absolute): a near-miss just makes it harder to
line a record up with its commit.

A commit-recording header is provisional while the work is in
progress; the *last* edit before `vc-x1 push` syncs it — and the
`## Done` entry / `[N]` anchor for that commit — to the final
commit title. See [Markdown anchor links](#markdown-anchor-links)
for the slug algorithm; the pre-commit checklist catches a
dangling `#anchor`, and a future `vc-x1 validate-repo` should too
(and should verify the recorded title matches the commit).

Existing sections and `## Done` entries (most of chores-01..09;
the pre-`0.48.1` `## Done` lines) predate this and keep their
free-form text; the convention applies going forward — `0.48.2`
converts the `0.48.1` section + its Done entry as the worked
example.

### Chores section content — no edit list; git is the record

A chores section is: a `Commits:` line (first line under the
header — see below), then [Prose form](#prose-form) (intro +
bullets) for what landed and why, and any `###` design
subsections. Bullets here are **conceptual** — design points,
structural notes — never a per-file edit list. That lives in the
commit message body, which is the source of truth for "what
changed mechanically" (immutable, `git show`-able, naturally
scoped to the commit). The chores section is the source of truth
for the design thinking; the two cross-link, neither restates the
other.

When the intro starts wanting to explain a mechanism,
hypothesis, or wrinkle, don't inflate it — promote that to its
own `###` subsection inside the same `chores-NN.md`. If the
wrinkle is a live design concern (something that *should*
change, not just be recorded), also add a `notes/todo.md` item
with a `[N]` ref pointing at that subsection (todo→chores is the
normal ref direction).

**Why:** a chores edit list and the commit body were specified
to be the same content in two places — and detail written twice
drifts. Git owns the mechanical record; chores owns the
narrative; `Commits:` links them.

### Chores commit references

The first line under a chores section header is a `Commits:`
line citing the git commit(s) that section records:

```
## refactor: port push to Context (0.48.0-6)

Commits: [[3]]

<intro paragraph...>
```

`Commits:` uses the file-local `[N]` reference machinery (see
[Reference numbering](#reference-numbering)),
**double-bracketed** so the brackets render — `Commits: [[3]]`,
or `Commits: [[3]],[[5]]` for several. (`[[3]]` shows as a
literal `[`, the `[3]` link, then a literal `]`; the inner
`[3]` resolves against its `[3]:` definition — CommonMark /
GitHub / VS Code all do this.) The `# References` definition
puts the **commit URL** as the destination, with the **full
40-hex SHA** in the title slot:

```
[3]: https://github.com/winksaville/vc-x1/commit/<12-hex> "<40-hex>"
```

- The 12-hex short SHA in the URL keeps it short; GitHub /
  GitLab resolve a unique prefix to the canonical commit page
  (GitLab's path has a `/-/` before `commit/`).
- The full SHA in the title is host-agnostic and unambiguous —
  it survives a repo host change, `git show <40-hex>` works in
  any clone, and external tooling scraping the notes (a
  database, say) gets the canonical identifier.

**Timing.** The commit doesn't exist when its chores section is
written, so the `Commits:` line is **backfilled when the next
change to that repo is started** — the cycle-start step grabs
the just-pushed commit's URL + SHA and fills it in. The single
newest section is briefly `Commits:`-less; that's fine — the
commit itself is the record, and `git log --grep "(X.Y.Z)"`
finds it.

## Cycle Protocol

Every change runs as a **cycle**: Preparation (`X.Y.Z-0`) →
Work commits (`X.Y.Z-1`, `X.Y.Z-2`, …) → Close-out (bare
`X.Y.Z`). Each commit runs through a per-commit flow whose
cargo cycle is `cargo fmt`, `cargo clippy --all-targets
-- -D warnings`, `cargo test`, `cargo install --path .
--locked` (skip-able for purely-docs commits; mandatory at
close-out). The full protocol — numbering, per-commit
flow, reviewing changes, close-out, pushing, ochid
trailers, sub-cycles — lives in
[`notes/cycle-protocol.md`](notes/cycle-protocol.md). Read
it before any commit work.


## Code Conventions

### Doc comments on every file, function, and method

Every `.rs` file must begin with a `//!` module docstring. Every
function and method must have a `///` doc comment. Keep them brief —
one sentence of purpose is often enough; the discipline is that the
comment exists, not that it be long. Doc comments follow the
[Prose form](#prose-form) shape (intro + bullets).

This is a deliberate override of the generic "write no comments"
default that applies to inline `//` comments. Doc comments on the
module / item surface are expected; inline explanatory comments
inside function bodies remain discouraged unless they capture a
non-obvious WHY.

**Clap-derive args:** doc comments on `#[arg(...)]` fields drive
`--help` output. Clap reflows by default and collapses bullets
into running prose. Add `#[arg(verbatim_doc_comment, ...)]` on
any field whose doc comment uses bullets so each `- …` lands on
its own line in the rendered help.

### `// OK: …` comments on `unwrap*` calls (Rust)

Non-test code that calls `.unwrap()`, `.unwrap_or(…)`,
`.unwrap_or_default()`, or `.unwrap_or_else(…)` must have a trailing
`// OK: …` comment that justifies why the call is acceptable.

- `// OK: <specific reason>` — document the real precondition,
  invariant, or domain reason. Preferred whenever the reason isn't
  self-evident.
- `// OK: obvious` — the default is self-evident from context (e.g.
  `desc.lines().next().unwrap_or("")` — empty desc → empty title).

Bare `// OK` is not used (reads like a truncated comment).
Abbreviations (e.g. `SE`) are not used because they require a decoder
ring for readers seeing the code out of context.

For provably-unreachable `.unwrap()` calls, also prefix with
`#[allow(clippy::unwrap_used)]` so the site stays silent if we enable
the project-wide `clippy::unwrap_used` lint later.

```rust
let max = stderr_level.unwrap_or(LevelFilter::Info); // OK: default verbosity when -v/-vv absent
let first_line = desc.lines().next().unwrap_or("");  // OK: obvious

match matches.len() {
    1 => {
        #[allow(clippy::unwrap_used)]
        // OK: `1 =>` arm guarantees matches.len() == 1
        Ok(TitleMatch::One(matches.into_iter().next().unwrap()))
    }
    // ...
}
```

Tests (`#[cfg(test)]`) are exempt — panicking on setup failure is the
correct test behavior.

### Ask for clarification on ambiguous input

When user input is ambiguous or missing necessary detail, stop and
ask a specific question. Do not proceed on a guess and hope the
result lands right — a clarifying question costs a few seconds;
redoing misaligned work costs much more.

### Recognize when stuck

If a simple task has eaten 5+ minutes of thinking or back-and-forth
without progress, stop. Summarize what's blocking — unclear
requirements, unfamiliar API surface, conflicting signals — and ask.
Continued flailing produces worse outcomes than a direct "I'm stuck
on X."

# Todo

This file contains near term tasks with a short description
and reference links to more details.

Intro paragraphs in `## Todo` and `## Bugs` should begin every
line with 1 leading space so they don't match the `^\d+\. `
pattern that locates numbered entries; 2 or 3 spaces also work.

## In Progress

When a `## Todo` item is picked up, its text moves here: the
problem overview and its list of things to do. That is followed
by the "plan" — a bulleted list of the development "ladder":
   - 0.xx.y-0 blah (done)
   - 0.xx.y-1 blah blah (current)
   - 0.xx.y-2 blah blah blah
   - 0.xx.y close-out and validation

_No cycle currently in progress._

## Todo

 Entries are in **strict priority rank** — #1 highest,
 descending. Reprioritize by moving an entry, then
 `vc-x1 fix-todo --no-dry-run notes/todo.md` to renumber.
 The numbers are positional rank, not stable IDs — to refer
 to a Todo, name it by its **title** (a greppable mention;
 a numbered list item has no anchor to link to), not its
 number. Long-tail entries
 live in [todo-backlog.md](todo-backlog.md). Use the
 [Prose Form in CLAUDE.md](/CLAUDE.md#prose-form); deeper
 detail goes in `notes/chores/chores-NN.md` design
 subsections (link via `[N]` ref).

1. **merge-non-ff recipe: codify in `notes/cycle-protocol.md`.**
   Multi-commit cycles default to the merge-non-ff trapezoid,
   but the recipe is split between `cycle-protocol.md` and
   CLAUDE.md with wrinkles re-derived each time (exercised
   manually on `0.62.0`). Codify the full recipe and
   standardize the flag spelling. Split out of Idea #1 (the
   broader cycle-protocol.md codification).
   - Recipe: `jj rebase -r <closeout> --onto <prev-closeout>
     --onto <work-tip>` (first `--onto` = trunk → first
     parent), then `jj new <merge>` to lift `@` above the
     merge, then `jj git push --bookmark main`.
   - Standardize on `--onto` / `-o` (canonical), not the
     `-d` alias — update CLAUDE.md's `-d` spellings (jj
     Basics + the post-amend `jj new` note) to `--onto`,
     plus a one-line "`-d` is an alias of `--onto`" so the
     docs don't read as two different flags.
   - `@` reverts to the work-tip's content until the
     `jj new` (the "post-amend `jj new`" gotcha already in
     CLAUDE.md).
   - Post-hoc caveat: if the cycle was already pushed
     keep-separate (as `0.62.0` was), the rebase needs
     `--ignore-immutable` and the push is a force-update of
     `main`; the standard recipe assumes the merge is set up
     before the close-out push.
2. **validate-numbering: rename the pair, check all

I'm acquainted. Here's the lay of the land:

## Project: `vc-x1`
A Rust CLI tool (`vc-x1`) whose own development is heavily process-driven — it provides `fix-todo` / `validate-todo` / `validate-repo` commands for managing the very `notes/` files that document it. Managed as **dual jj+git repos** (app at root, bot session under `.claude`). Bot instructions just moved from `CLAUDE.md` → **`AGENTS.md`** (read it — it's the source of truth for conventions: cycle protocol, prose form, notes/chores/todo formats, doc-comment + `// OK:` rules).

## Current working state (uncommitted)
HEAD is **detached** at `52bf5e0` (`docs: strict-rank todos, drop Priorities (0.62.1)`), latest cycle just closed out. jj `@` is `d333f0ca` with two uncommitted changes:

1. **`CLAUDE.md → AGENTS.md`** — pure rename (0-byte content diff). Note this isn't yet reflected *inside* the file: line 1 still reads `# CLAUDE.md - Bot Instructions` and it self-references `/CLAUDE.md#prose-form` in several places (e.g. `todo.md:32`). If the rename is intentional, those internal references need updating too.

2. **`notes/todo.md`** — looks like a **stray/accidental edit**, worth a second look before it gets committed:
   - A new `0.` numbered entry inserted at the top of `## Ideas` (line ~204), whose body is a near-verbatim copy of text that already lives in the `## Todo` intro ("Numbering helps a human orient in a long list…").
   - Introduces trailing whitespace on the preceding blank line.
   - A `0.` rank is also odd given the "#1 highest" strict-rank convention.

_No cycle currently in progress_ per `todo.md`. The top-ranked Todo is **"merge-non-ff recipe: codify in `notes/cycle-protocol.md`"**.

What would you like to do — start a cycle on a todo, sort out the AGENTS.md rename + todo.md edit, or something else?

Good — and yes, the empirical signal is worth flagging: **this session, AGENTS.md was *not* auto-loaded.** The only thing injected under `claudeMd` was your `MEMORY.md`. Claude Code still auto-loads `CLAUDE.md`, not `AGENTS.md` — so the bare rename you have staged is a quiet regression for *me*: I'd start every session blind to the conventions in that file. (Zed picks up AGENTS.md; Claude Code doesn't, at least in this build.)

lastPrompt: I use zed and it now defaults to AGENTS.md, so I was wondering if you'd notice, you did. I'd like to move to using AGENTS.md instead of CLAUDE.md, thoughts

content.text:
Big picture: I think moving to AGENTS.md is the right call — it's the emerging cross-tool standard and Zed/Codex/others key off it. The only real wrinkle is the one this session already demonstrated:

**Claude Code does not auto-load AGENTS.md — only CLAUDE.md.** So a bare rename (what's staged now) means every Claude Code session starts without your conventions. We want one canonical file that *both* tools see.

Three ways to get there:

- **Symlink `CLAUDE.md` → `AGENTS.md`** (AGENTS.md is the real file). Content lives once; Zed reads AGENTS.md natively, Claude Code reads CLAUDE.md and the OS resolves it to the same bytes. This repo already uses symlinks idiomatically (the `.claude` dir is one), and it's zero-duplication. My recommendation.
- **`CLAUDE.md` = one line `@AGENTS.md`** (an import). More explicit/visible in a diff, no symlink semantics, but it's a second real file and leans on Claude Code's `@`-import resolution.
- **Pure rename, AGENTS.md only.** Cleanest tree, but I lose auto-load until Claude Code ships AGENTS.md support. Regression today.

Either of the first two keeps both tools working with a single source of truth.

Separately, the rename implies a **reference cleanup** — `grep` finds `CLAUDE.md` mentions in two buckets:

- **Live surfaces to update:** `AGENTS.md` itself (title line 1 still says `# CLAUDE.md`, plus self-refs at 65–79), `README.md`, `notes/README.md`, `ARCHITECTURE.md`, `notes/cycle-protocol.md`, `notes/todo.md` (incl. the `[0]: /CLAUDE.md#prose-form` ref), and one comment in `src/push.rs:880`.
- **History to leave alone:** all of `notes/chores/chores-*.md` and `notes/done.md` — those are append-mostly records of what CLAUDE.md *was* at the time; rewriting them would falsify the record (your own no-edit-history convention).

This is a docs change, so per your protocol it'd run as a cycle. Before I plan that out, two decisions:

message.content: If we use a symlink do the markdown links to anchors (not sure the actual terms, help) automatically continue to work?

content.text:
Good question, and the terms are worth pinning down since they make the answer precise:

A link like `[Prose form](../CLAUDE.md#prose-form)` has two parts:
- **Link destination** (the path): `../CLAUDE.md` — what file to open.
- **Fragment** (a.k.a. URL fragment / hash): `#prose-form` — which points at an **anchor**, the auto-generated **slug** of a heading (`## Prose form` → `prose-form`).

So your question splits in two, and they have different answers:

**The fragment/slug half is fine.** `#prose-form` keeps matching because the *heading text* is unchanged — it's now in AGENTS.md, but `## Prose form` still slugs to `prose-form`. No fragment breaks from the rename itself.

**The path half — resolving through the symlink — depends on where it's rendered:**

- **Local editor (Zed/VS Code, cmd-click):** works. You click `CLAUDE.md#prose-form`, the OS transparently resolves the symlink to AGENTS.md, the editor opens it and jumps to the heading. ✅
- **GitHub/GitLab web:** *breaks.* A symlinked `.md` isn't rendered as markdown on GitHub — it's shown as a little "symlink → AGENTS.md" stub page. So clicking the link lands on the stub, the markdown never renders, and the `#prose-form` anchor has nothing to scroll to. ❌

So the honest answer: **no, not automatically everywhere.** The symlink fixes Claude Code's auto-load (it just needs *a file named `CLAUDE.md`* to exist), but it does **not** make the existing `CLAUDE.md#anchor` links work on GitHub.

The clean consequence: the symlink is purely a **compatibility shim for Claude Code's loader** — nothing human-facing should point *at* it. Every markdown cross-reference should be repointed to the real file, `AGENTS.md#anchor`. Then the links work in editors *and* on GitHub, and don't depend on the symlink at all.

That reframes the second decision: updating the live references isn't really optional cleanup — it's what makes the links robust. The symlink and the reference sweep are complementary, not alternatives:
- symlink (or `@AGENTS.md` import) → Claude Code keeps auto-loading.
- repoint live `CLAUDE.md#…` links → `AGENTS.md#…` → links work everywhere.

Want me to go with that combined approach (symlink + repoint live refs, leave chores/done history alone), or would you still rather keep it to the mechanism only for now?

content.text:
Good — that's a clean combo: `CLAUDE.md` = `@AGENTS.md` (one line) keeps Claude Code auto-loading, AGENTS.md is the single real file, and every link points at the real file so it resolves in editors *and* on GitHub.

One consequence to decide before I scope the work. With the `@import` approach, `CLAUDE.md` becomes a one-line stub with **no headings** — so *any* `CLAUDE.md#anchor` link now dangles (the anchor lives in AGENTS.md). The mentions fall into three buckets:

1. **Live anchor links** — must repoint, or they break:
   - `README.md` (880–890), `notes/README.md` (42–49), `notes/cycle-protocol.md` (58,66,75,223), `notes/todo.md` (32 + the `[0]:` def at 350), `ARCHITECTURE.md` (19,195).
2. **AGENTS.md itself** — title line 1 + self-refs (65–79).
3. **Live prose mentions** (not links, just the name) — `notes/todo-backlog.md`, `notes/cargo-locked-issue.md`, `notes/draft-reviews.md`, `notes/bot-data-formats.md`, `src/push.rs:880` (comment), `README.md:604`. Update for accuracy going forward.

Then **history** — `notes/chores/chores-*.md` and `notes/done.md`. Mostly prose like "CLAUDE.md: add X", which was *true at the time* — rewriting it would falsify the record, against your convention, so I'd leave those. **But** a few historical files carry live navigational anchor links in their boilerplate headers (e.g. `chores-10/11/12` open with ``[`CLAUDE.md`](../../CLAUDE.md#reference-numbering)``). Those point at the *current* convention doc, so they'd dangle too.

So the one policy call on history:

That breaks the decision cleanly. The 154 is really three piles:

| Pile | Count | What it is |
|---|---|---|
| **History prose** (chores-*/done.md) | ~109 | "CLAUDE.md: add X" records — true at the time |
| **History anchor links** (chores-10/11/12 headers) | 3 | live nav pointers to the current convention doc |
| **Live mentions** (everything else) | 42 | 13 anchor links + 29 prose, all describing the *current* system |

(The 112 history total = ~109 prose + 3 links; one of chores-12's two `#` hits is the `:158` literal, which isn't a link.)

My recommendation — a single defensible rule rather than picking line-by-line:

- **Live files → repoint everything (all 42)**, links *and* prose. These docs (`README.md`, `ARCHITECTURE.md`, `notes/README.md`, `cycle-protocol.md`, `todo.md`, `todo-backlog.md`, `cargo-locked-issue.md`, `draft-reviews.md`, `bot-data-formats.md`, `por-dual-parity-audit.md`, `init-clone-refactor-conflict.md`, `AGENTS.md` itself + `src/push.rs:880`) describe the system *as it is now*, so they should name the real file, `AGENTS.md`.
- **History files → touch only the 3 anchor links** (chores-10:12, chores-11:6, chores-12:6) so they don't dangle; leave the ~109 prose mentions and the `chores-12:158` literal exactly as written — that's the historical record.

Net: ~45 line edits across ~13 live files + 3 surgical link fixes in history. The rule is easy to state in the commit body and easy to validate later ("live names AGENTS.md; history untouched except resolving links").

That's a docs change, so I'd run it as a proper cycle. **Shall I read `cycle-protocol.md`, pick the next version, and lay out the ladder** (likely: prep → mechanism+AGENTS.md self → live repoint → history link fixes → close-out)? Say go and I'll start the Preparation step.
